items.briefing:
With the end of financial year here, Australian staff should be extra cautious about phishing scams. Cybercriminals commonly exploit tax time to send convincing fake emails impersonating the ATO or other organisations. Security researchers are urging Australians to use phishing-resistant authentication methods — such as passkeys or hardware security keys — rather than relying on passwords or SMS codes alone, as these offer much stronger protection against fraud.

Separately, Barracuda has flagged a rise in phishing attacks and malware targeting Microsoft users, so be especially wary of unexpected emails asking you to click links or open attachments.

**Tip:** If you receive any EOFY-related emails asking you to log in or provide details, go directly to the official website instead of clicking any links in the email.

featured.title: Phishing-resistant authentication can prevent tax-time fraud
featured.quote: Tax-time fraud exploiting stolen credentials poses direct risk to Australian taxpayers and ATO systems.
